name: Necessary Good (x1) Demand Curves, Four Different Micro Foundations

book_quotes.quote: Figures 3.10 and 3.11 compare the theoretically expected demand curves of the necessary and luxury goods to the actual curves in the four simulation models. In the interest of saving space, cross demand curves and Engel curves are not displayed. All actual elasticities are listed in table 3.1. It is evident that the very different micro foundations of the various models have essentially no effect on the aggregate results.

All simulations were undertaken in NetLogo, and the programs for the various models can be made available on request. For the sake of comparison, all models have the same fixed total income ($1,000,000), population (5,000), minimum level of necessary consumption ($10) average income per capita ($200), and initial prices p1 = 1 and p2 = 2. The income distribution is set initially as a log normal distribution with a given minimum income ($50).

The demand curve for x1 is generated by raising its price from 1 to 1.5 by increments of 0.01, while for that for x2 is generated by similarly raising its price from 2 to 3. Nominal income is held constant as each price ultimately increases by 50%, which means that real income (y/p1) ultimately falls by a corresponding amount.

primary_source.publication: Shaikh, Anwar. 2016. Capitalism: Competition, Conflict, Crises. New York: Oxford University Press.
primary_source.table_or_series_id: Figure 3.10 (p. 99) and Table 3.1 (p. 100) — NetLogo simulation output from four micro-foundations models (Neoclassical Homogeneous, Neoclassical Heterogeneous, Whimsical, Imitate-Innovate) plus the theoretical curve
primary_source.units: p1 ∈ [0.80, 1.60] vs x1 ∈ [70, 110] (model-currency price; model-units quantity)
primary_source.license: Book figure; NetLogo programs 'available on request' per footnote 21 (Amr Ragab acknowledged for implementation); reproduction governed by OUP permissions
primary_source.available_through: Book PDF in Inputs/Capitalism Data/; NetLogo source code not publicly archived as of 2026

components.label: Theoretical curve
components.description: Analytic demand curve from eq. (3.5) with c = 0.5, x1min = 10, p2 = 2, y = 200; price p1 swept 1.0 → 1.5.

components.label: Neoclassical Homogeneous Agent simulation
components.description: Cobb-Douglas utility, α = β so c = 0.5; 5,000 identical agents.

components.label: Neoclassical Heterogeneous Agent simulation
components.description: Each agent has distinct Cobb-Douglas with c drawn uniform[0,1] (theoretical mean 0.5); each agent repeats identical behaviour each period.

components.label: Whimsical Agent simulation
components.description: Each agent draws a fresh c ~ Uniform[0,1] in every period; mirrors Becker (1962) impulsive consumer.

components.label: Imitate-Innovate Agent simulation
components.description: 80% of agents adapt c toward local neighbourhood mean (reaction coef. ~ Uniform[0,1]); 20% randomly mutate c. Inspired by Dosi et al. (1999, sec. 4).

methodology_notes: Fig 3.10 plots five curves on the same axes: one theoretical analytical curve (from eq 3.5) plus four NetLogo simulation outputs. Series is fundamentally a simulation/illustration, not historical data.
Simulation parameters are exhaustively stated on p. 99 and in Table 3.1 note: y = 200; π = 0.50 [discretionary propensity]; x1min = 10; p1 = 1; p2 = 2.
The NetLogo programs are 'available on request' (footnote 21, p. 99); they are not (as of 2026) deposited in a public repository. Re-implementation is feasible from the verbal model descriptions.
Stub content_type='time_series' is incorrect for a simulation experiment; re-typed to 'theoretical'.
Construction set to 'composite' because the figure overlays five distinct generating processes.
open_questions: Stub content_type was 'time_series'; re-typed to 'theoretical'. Phase 4 should confirm.

NetLogo source code is described as 'available on request' (Amr Ragab acknowledged); has the code ever been publicly archived? If not, reproduction requires implementing the four models from the verbal specifications in §III.5.
Random seeds are not stated; exact reproduction of the printed curves is therefore impossible — only statistical reproduction up to Monte-Carlo error.

review_history.notes: Populated from book pp. 96–100. NetLogo simulation output; re-typed time_series → theoretical.
